writer.name: 帕特里克·罗特曼 Patrick Rotman

director.name: 帕特里克·罗特曼 Patrick Rotman

introduction: Through poignant archives, Patrick Rotman traces the history of the thirty-five thousand volunteers from all over the world who fought Francoism in the Spanish Civil War. During the Spanish War (1936–1939), volunteers flocked from all over the world to defend the young republic, threatened by the Franco putsch which was supported from the outset by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy. Parisian, Czech and British workers, New York dockworkers, Polish or English miners, German and Italian anti-fascist activists...: in all, some 35 thousand men, from some fifty countries fought, often on the front line and without any formation, in increasingly desperate battles. They responded to the call made under the auspices of Moscow by the Communist International, when Western democracies decided not to intervene. They also heard the pleas of André Malraux and George Orwell, the French writer at the head of an aerial squadron, the Englishman within the Poum (Unified Marxist, Anti-Stalin). They may also have seen the incredible images that a couple of young photographers, Gerta Pohorylle and Endre Friedmann, alias Gerda Taro and Robert Capa, sent day by day from the front. Like them, writers Ernest Hemingway, John Dos Passos, Gustave Regler, and documentary filmmaker Joris Ivens helped exalt the heroic resistance of the Spanish people in the face of a much larger Francoist army. Also at their side, the Pravda correspondent, Mikhail Koltsov, reports to the NKVD...Restoring both individual destinies and historical complexity, Patrick Rotman traces these three years of a lost struggle, the facts of which continue to inspire revolutionary romanticism today. This is an intense and detailed account, from two poignant archives, including many of the magnificent images of Robert Capa and Gerda Taro, who died before the final defeat at the age of 27, accidentally crushed by a Republican tank. "
